name: Palmer Hill Mine
short_description: Historic New York garnet mine in the Adirondack foothills. Search for red garnets, feldspar, and mica specimens in this classic northeastern collecting site.
description:
Palmer Hill Mine is a historic garnet and feldspar locality in Clinton County, located in the foothills of the Adirondack Mountains. The site occupies relatively flat terrain at 1,147 feet elevation, making it accessible for rockhounds of varying skill levels. The mine historically produced garnet specimens sought by collectors, and the surrounding country rock exposes pegmatitic and metamorphic minerals typical of the Adirondack region.

This locality sits within the ancient Proterozoic bedrock of the Adirondack Mountains, where intense metamorphism during the Grenville Orogeny (roughly 1.3 billion years ago) created the mineral-rich gneisses and pegmatites that characterize the region. Garnet formed under high temperature and pressure conditions, often in association with mica-rich layers. The feldspar and mica specimens reflect the granitic pegmatite dikes common to Adirondack geology.

Visitors should expect mixed success depending on site conditions and prior collecting activity. The relatively flat topography means less strenuous hiking compared to steeper Adirondack locations, but ground cover and overgrowth may limit exposure of fresh material. Spring and fall offer the most pleasant collecting conditions in upstate New York, with moderate temperatures and reduced insect activity.

This is a private or historically worked site, so access and collection rules may be restricted. Always verify current land ownership and obtain permission before collecting. The location is moderately remote and cell service should not be relied upon for emergencies.
history: Palmer Hill Mine is a historic garnet locality developed during the 19th and early 20th centuries when New York garnet specimens were prized by collectors and lapidaries. The site's garnets formed during Proterozoic metamorphism in the Adirondack Mountains, when regional tectonism drove temperatures and pressures high enough to crystallize almandine garnet in metamorphic host rocks and pegmatitic intrusions.
tips: Search along exposed outcrops and the mine waste piles for garnet crystals, typically displaying dodecahedral and trapezohedral faces. Look for reddish-brown to deep red garnet specimens, often ranging from pea-sized to marble-sized crystals embedded in light-colored feldspar and quartz. Bring a 10x hand lens to identify garnet's cubic crystal forms and distinguish it from similar-looking minerals. A small chisel and hammer work well for carefully extracting specimens from host rock, and a geologic hammer is useful for breaking apart pegmatite boulders to expose fresh garnet pockets.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain at 1,147 feet elevation in the Adirondack foothills. Mixed forest cover with occasional exposed bedrock, pegmatite outcrops, and old mine tailings. Walking distance from parking area to prime collecting zones is typically less than 0.5 miles. Ground may be soft or boggy in spring; footwear should be sturdy and waterproof. Vegetation can obscure mineralized zones, especially in summer and early fall.
best_season: May - September. Spring and early summer offer the best combination of accessible ground conditions and comfortable temperatures in the northeastern Adirondacks. Avoid late fall through early spring when snow, ice, and muddy terrain make access difficult.
written_directions: From the Town of Black Brook, proceed north on County Road toward the Palmer Hill area. The site is situated in forested terrain approximately 2-3 miles northeast of the town center. Use GPS coordinates (44.465786, -73.674403) to navigate to the locality. Note that roads in the area are rural and may be unpaved in sections; a detailed topographic map and GPS device are strongly recommended. Access is via private land, so obtain permission and directions from local landowners before visiting.
road_conditions: Unpaved rural roads leading to site. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Roads may be muddy or impassable in wet seasons.
parking_notes: Limited informal parking near site access point. No designated parking lot. Space for 2-3 vehicles at pull-off areas near road.
collection_rules: Private land - permission from landowner required before collecting. Collection is limited to hand tools only; no mechanized excavation permitted. Respect site boundaries and avoid damaging structures or leaving debris. Pack out all waste material.
rules:
Permission from landowner required
Hand tools only, no motorized equipment
Respect property boundaries
No commercial collection
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Town of Black Brook has limited services. Larger towns with gas, food, and lodging include Chateaugay (approximately 5 miles) and Peru (approximately 8 miles).
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with minimal elevation gain, accessible to most hikers with moderate fitness. Unpaved roads to parking area require careful navigation. Uneven ground, scattered rocks, and mine tailings present minor trip hazards. Sturdy waterproof footwear strongly recommended, especially in wet seasons. No wheelchair access due to unpaved roads and uneven ground.

faq.question: What garnets can I find at Palmer Hill Mine in New York?
faq.answer: Palmer Hill Mine is known for almandine garnet specimens, typically deep red to reddish-brown in color. Garnets range from small pea-sized crystals to larger marble-sized specimens, often displaying dodecahedral crystal forms embedded in light feldspar and quartz matrix. Quality and size vary depending on recent collecting activity and site conditions.

faq.question: Do I need permission to rockhound at Palmer Hill Mine?
faq.answer: Yes. Palmer Hill Mine is located on private land, so you must obtain written permission from the current landowner before collecting. Contact local town authorities in Black Brook or work with regional rockhounding clubs to identify and reach the property owner.

faq.question: What tools work best for collecting at Palmer Hill Mine?
faq.answer: Hand tools only are permitted at this site. A geological hammer, chisel, 10x hand lens, and small pry bar are most effective for extracting garnet specimens from host rock and pegmatite outcrops. Avoid heavy machinery or powered tools, which are prohibited on private land.

faq.question: What is the best season to rockhound near Chateaugay, New York?
faq.answer: May through September is the best collecting season at Palmer Hill Mine. Spring and early summer offer comfortable temperatures, reduced snow and ice, and more accessible ground conditions. Late fall through early spring brings muddy terrain, snow cover, and challenging access on unpaved roads.

faq.question: Is Palmer Hill Mine beginner-friendly for rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, the flat terrain and relatively short walks from parking make this site suitable for beginners and families with children ages 8 and up. However, you must arrange landowner permission in advance and be prepared to identify garnets and other minerals on site.

faq.question: What other minerals occur with garnets at Palmer Hill Mine?
faq.answer: Feldspar, quartz, and mica commonly occur alongside garnet at Palmer Hill Mine. These minerals formed together in pegmatitic intrusions and metamorphic gneisses during the Proterozoic Grenville mountain-building event. Searching through feldspar-rich pegmatite boulders often yields the best garnet specimens.

meta_title: Palmer Hill Mine - Garnet Rockhounding in New York
meta_description: Collect red almandine garnets, feldspar, and mica at Palmer Hill Mine in Clinton County, NY. Private land access. Historic Adirondack locality.
